coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON heterojunctions for broad spectrum photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Jungang
Cheng, Huijie
Yang, Chao
Takeda, Osamu
Zhu, Hongmin
coredata.dc:description: There remains a pressing challenge in the efficient utilization of broad spectrum solar energy in the photocatalytic applications. Here we design and fabricate carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON (CQDs/H-γ-TaON) hollow urchin heterojunctions that can enhance the UV–visible–NIR broad spectrum active photocatalytic property. Novel CQDs/H-γ-TaON photocatalysts have harnessed UV, visible, and near-infrared light to decompose organic contaminants in aqueous solution. Moreover, efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production as high as 496.5µmolh−1 with an apparent quantum efficiency of 12.2% under 420nm (about 61 times higher than that of conventional TaON) was achieved over CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunction, corresponding the order of hydrogen production rate: CQDs/H-γ-TaON>H-γ-TaON>γ-TaON. The improved UV and visible photocatalytic property can be attributed to high surface area from hollow nanostructures and efficient charge transfer based photoluminescence, electrochemical impedance and Mott–Schottky analysis. Especially, the NIR photocatalytic activity from the synergistic effects of the hydrogenation and the decoration of CQDs is ascribed to the efficient charge separation and transfer and up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs that absorb NIR light and convert into visible light and transfer to visible-light photocatalytic H-γ-TaON. The CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunctions may open up more opportunities in the design of efficient broad spectrum active photocatalysts for environmental remediation and clean energy generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogenation
TaON
Heterojunctions
Photocatalysts

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of hierarchical CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) evolution of XRD patterns associated with a certain theta degree of the samples, (i) γ-TaON, (ii) H-γ-TaON and (iii) CQDs/H-γ-TaON.
SEM (a), TEM (d, e and g) images and schematic illustration (f) of CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures as well as TEM images of H-γ-TaON (b) and CQDs (c).
XPS spectra of (i) γ-TaON, (ii) H-γ-TaON and (iii) CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures. (a) Full survey, (b) Ta 4f spectra, (c) Ta 4p and N 1s spectra and (d) O 1s spectra.
(a) C 1s XPS and (b) Raman spectra of CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Ta2O5 and γ-TaON, (b) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures, and (c) down-converted and (d) up-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) Nyquist plots and (c) Mott–Schottky plots of (i) γ-TaON, (ii) H-γ-TaON and (iii) CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures as well as (d) Mott–Schottky plots of CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures at frequencies of 200, 500, 1000Hz in NaOH solution.

Photocatalytic degradation of (a–c) RhB and (d–f) AO7 in the absence of catalyst (i) and the presence of (ii) γ-TaON, (iii) H-γ-TaON and (iv) CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures under (a, d) UV, (b, e) visible, and (c, f) NIR light irradiation.

(a) Photocatalytic hydrogen generation of β-TaON, γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ- TaON heterostructures and (b) recycling runs for the photocatalytic hydrogen generation of CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures.
Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures under UV–visible–NIR light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON heterojunctions for broad spectrum photocatalytic performance

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There remains a pressing challenge in the efficient utilization of broad spectrum solar energy in the photocatalytic applications. Here we design and fabricate carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON (CQDs/H-γ-TaON) hollow urchin heterojunctions that can enhance the UV–visible–NIR broad spectrum active photocatalytic property. Novel CQDs/H-γ-TaON photocatalysts have harnessed UV, visible, and near-infrared light to decompose organic contaminants in aqueous solution. Moreover, efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production as high as 496.5µmolh−1 with an apparent quantum efficiency of 12.2% under 420nm (about 61 times higher than that of conventional TaON) was achieved over CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunction, corresponding the order of hydrogen production rate: CQDs/H-γ-TaON>H-γ-TaON>γ-TaON. The improved UV and visible photocatalytic property can be attributed to high surface area from hollow nanostructures and efficient charge transfer based photoluminescence, electrochemical impedance and Mott–Schottky analysis. Especially, the NIR photocatalytic activity from the synergistic effects of the hydrogenation and the decoration of CQDs is ascribed to the efficient charge separation and transfer and up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs that absorb NIR light and convert into visible light and transfer to visible-light photocatalytic H-γ-TaON. The CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunctions may open up more opportunities in the design of efficient broad spectrum active photocatalysts for environmental remediation and clean energy generation.

fx1
Hierarchical carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterojunctions exhibit excellent UV–visible–NIR broad spectrum active photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy supply and related environmental issues are among the biggest technological challenges being confronted by chemists and technologists in the 21st century [1]. Renewable, clean and carbon-neutral alternative energy resources is urgently needed to limit our dependence on fossil fuels. Solar energy is widely accepted as a free, abundant and endlessly renewable source of clean energy. It is highly desirable for the harvest and conversion of solar energy into a usable energy form by the use of photocatalytic processes [2–6].

Since TiO2 material was used to split water into hydrogen and oxygen [7], many metal oxides have received immense attention for the purpose of efficient degradation of harmful organic substances and hydrogen production through splitting water [5,6]. However, different (oxy)nitrides, such as TaON, Ta3N5, SrNbO2N, LaTiO2N and GaN:ZnO have also been found to be visible light responsive photocatalysts [8–20]. Especially, tantalum oxynitride with the suitable band gap and band position, is considered for solar energy utilization [8–13]. Among the Ta–O–N system, γ-TaON, as a metastable polymorph phase, is less reported in comparison with β-TaON [21]. However, the serious recombination of the photogenerated charges is definitely detrimental to the photocatalytic performance of oxynitrides. Thus, it is essential to guide the design and fabrication of high performance oxynitrides.

Generally, photocatalyst features such as high crystallinity, large surface area and efficient charge transfer, usually enhance the photocatalytic performance [2–6]. It is well-known that nanostructured engineering could be employed to improve the photocatalytic performance [22]. Especially, hollow micro-/nanostructures with controllable size, shape, composition and interior architecture are attracting interests due to their intriguing properties and widespread applications [23,24]. For examples, (Ga1−x Znx)(N1−x O x ) hollow spheres exhibit excellent catalytic activity for overall water splitting, and the highest quantum efficiency of (Ga1−x Zn x )(N1−x O x ) is as high as 17.3% under visible light irradiation [25]. LaTiO2N hollow sphere are synthesized by use of carbon spheres as template and the quantum efficiency for H2 generation can be as high as 3.4% under visible light irradiation [26]. Ta3N5 quantum dots coupled TaON hollow spheres by an in-situ chemical reduction route exhibited an excellent rate of oxygen production with an apparent quantum efficiency of 67% under 420nm [27]. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop a new modification approach that can enhance the photocatalytic activity of oxynitride nanostructures.

To optimize the photocatalytic activity, many efforts have been focused on enhancing light absorption by narrowing the band gap of semiconductors, but it is still a challenge to improve the visible-light absorption and energy conversion [28–32]. Frankly, hydrogen-treatment is favorable to eliminate the recombination centers of light-induced electrons and holes of semiconductors [29,30]. For instances, the photocatalytic activities of hydrogen-treated TiO2 and WO3 nanostructures can be enhanced as a result of increased donor density by introducing a moderate amount of oxygen vacancies [30,31]. Core/shell structured hydrogen-doped black titania with high light absorption and low charge recombination presented the excellent photocatalytic activity for the photo-oxidation of organic molecules in water and the hydrogen evolution [32]. Therefore, hydrogen treatment is a simple strategy that can significantly enhance the photocatalytic property of oxynitride nanostructures.

In view of solar energy utilization, semiconductor photocatalyst that can harvest the wide spectrum of solar light, from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) wavelength, and achieve efficient solar energy conversion, remains one of the most challenging missions [33]. In order to utilize solar energy efficiently, great efforts have been achieved to develop UV–visible–NIR active catalysts [33–36]. Nevertheless, there is a big space for the utilization of the near-infrared light which accounts for 44% of the solar spectrum [31]. However, near-infrared light photocatalysts have focused mainly on up-conversion materials, such as Bi2WO6, Cu2(OH)PO4, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [33–37]. Especially, CQDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays, core–shell CQDs/silicon nanowire array, CQDs/Ag3PO4 and CQDs/C3N4 composite as well as CQDs/Cu2O heterostructures exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity and structural stability over the photodecomposition of organic compounds, hydrogen evolution of water splitting and solar-light-driven conversion of CO2 to methanol [38–42]. The conjugated π structure of CQDs in these composites plays an important role for their improved photocatalytic activities. Since CQDs possess the potential for both up- and down-converted photoluminescence, as well as photoinduced electron transfer properties, which make CQDs potential cocatalyst candidates, capable of harnessing a broader range of visible or near-infrared light to enhance the performance of the main photocatalyst [37–42]. Thus, abroad spectrum (UV–vis–NIR) light active photocatalyst with the strong chemical interaction between the CQDs and oxynitride nanostructures could be a high priority for practical photocatalytic materials. Despite the advances in this field, there is no report for the wide range possibilities that simultaneous consideration of efficient charge separation and stability by novel carbon quantum dots coupled hydrogenated-semiconductor heterojunction design offers more opportunities in the design of the UV–vis–NIR-active composites in energy conversion field.

Herein, we develop for the first time a novel CQDs/hydrogenated-semiconductor heterojunction design for efficient solar-driven photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production from water splitting in which hydrogen-treated γ-TaON hollow urchins supported with an optimized combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs/H-γ-TaON) are used as the synergetic catalysts. To be successful, the pivot improvement is the effectiveness of this novel photocatalyst owing to the following strategies: (i) possessing high surface area of γ-TaON hollow urchins that enhance the contact between charge carriers and surface species; (ii) developing a promising strategy of hydrogenated-γ-TaON hollow urchins with efficient charge transfer; and (iii) achieving UV–vis–NIR-active CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterojunction with efficient charge separation and transfer to achieve the excellent broad spectrum photocatalytic performance.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. In a typical procedure, 1g tantalum powder was added to 20mL of 1.33molL−1 hydrofluoric acid aqueous solution with 4.0mL of 30wt% hydrogen peroxide, heated at 240°C for 12h in a Teflon-lined cylindrical autoclave. After cooling naturally, the tantalum oxide was separated, washed with ethanol for three times and then dried in oven at 60°C for 12h. Especially, β-TaON and γ-TaON samples were prepared by nitridation heat treatment of Ta2O5 powders according to our previous procedure [13,43]. In a typical synthesis of hydrogenated-γ-TaON urchins, 0.2g γ-TaON hollow sample was heated in tube furnace filled with hydrogen and argon gas mixtures (1:1) at 800°C for 3h.

Firstly, CQDs were synthesized by a facile one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonic treatment. In a typical experiment, 0.05mol glucose was dissolved in 50mL deionized water to form a clear solution. Then 50mL NaOH solution (1mol/L) was added to the solution of glucose, and the mixed solution was submitted to a 150W (40kHz) ultrasonic treatment for 2h at room temperature. Then, the raw solution obtained from glucose/NaOH was adjusted to pH=7 with HCl. After that, the crude solution underwent dialysis treatment using a semipermeable membrane to remove the impurities. After filter treatment, the obtained solution was brown, implying the formation of CQDs. Secondly, with regard to the synthesis of CQDs/H-γ-TaON, 0.1g H-γ-TaON hollow urchins were added into 50mL CQDs aqueous solution. Then the heterostructures were obtained via an oil bath reflux at 90°C for 3h. The CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunctions were washed with water, and then dried in an oven at 60°C for 12h.

The obtained products were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, MAC Science Co. Ltd., Japan) using Cu Kα (λ=0.1546nm) and XRD patterns were obtained at 10–90° by step scanning with a step size of 0.02°. The morphology and size of the resultant products were observed using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL, JSM-6701F) with energy-dispersive spectra and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra (DRS) of the samples were recorded on a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Cary 5000, Varian) with an integrating sphere attachment within the range of 200–1800nm and with BaSO4 as the reflectance standard. The chemical states of the sample were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in a VG Multilab 2009 system (UK) with a monochromatic Al Kα source and charge neutralizer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out on a Horiba JobinYvon (Fluoro Max4) Luminescence Spectrometer.

For the γ-TaON, hydrogenated-γ-TaON, CQDs/hydrogenated-γ-TaON heterojunctions as the various catalysts (0.1g) were chosen for the evaluation of photocatalytic activity. Photocatalytic activity was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and acid orange 7 (AO7) in aqueous solution. A cylindrical Pyrex flask (200mL) was placed in a sealed black box of which the top was open and the cutoff filter was set on the window face of the reaction vessel to ensure the desired irradiation condition. In each experiments, the samples as catalysts were added RhB and AO7 solution (10−4 M, 100mL). A 350W mercury lamp with a maximum emission at 356nm was used as the UV source for photocatalysis. A 300W Xe arc lamp was used as the visible light source with filter glasses to filter UV light for visible light photocatalysis. A 250W infrared lamp used as the NIR light source where the λ=760nm were filtered out during NIR light photocatalysis. Before illumination, the suspension between photocatalyst powders and RhB and AO7 at given time intervals (3mL aliquots) were sampled and centrifuged to remove photocatalysts.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production was carried out in an air-free closed gas circulation system reaction cell made of quartz. The total cylindrical volume of the cell was 250mL. Hydrogen evolution was detected using a gas chromatograph (GC-3240, TCD, Ar carrier), which was directly connected to the system. For oxynitrides, the Ru cocatalyst was loaded on the photocatalysts by impregnation from an (NH4)2RuCl6 aqueous solution followed by a hydrogen reduction treatment at 200°C for 5h. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 0.3g of catalyst was dispersed in a 200mL mixed solution of 20mL methanol and 180mL DI water (pH=7). The reaction was initiated by irradiation with a 300W Xe lamp fitted with a cutoff filter (λ>420nm).

An apparent quantum efficiency (QE) measurement was carried out using a Pyrex type reaction vessel and a 300W xenon lamp fitted with a 420nm band-pass filter. The number of incident photons was calibrated with a Si photodiode (FZ-A, HANDY). The QE was calculated according to the following equation:
QE
( % )
=
The
rate
of
reacted
electrons
The
rate
of
incident
photons
× 100 % =
The
rate
of
evolved
H
2

× 2
The
rate
of
incident
photons
× 100 %

The photoelectrodes of γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures on a FTO conducting glass were fabricated by using electrophoretic deposition (Supporting information). Mott–Schottky analyses were performed with a Solartron frequency response analyzer under control by ZPLOT software. A solution containing 0.5M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. A 300W Xe lamp was utilized as a light source. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of samples were measured by an electrochemical analyzer (Solartron1287) with a 10mV amplitude perturbation and frequencies between 0.1Hz and 10MHz.

The realization of this tentative design needs feasible and straightforward procedures. The schematic illustration for the fabrication process of novel CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures is presented in Figure 1. Firstly, the fluorine-containing Ta2O5 (F–Ta2O5) hollow urchins were prepared through an in situ hydrothermal treatment process. Then, γ-TaON hollow urchins were obtained via the thermal nitridation route. After the hydrogenation, H-γ-TaON hollow urchins were produced under heat-treatment in hydrogen and argon gases. Finally, the carbon quantum dots decorated H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterojunctions were achieved by the oil bath reflux process, depicting the sketch of hierarchical CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures.

The phase of γ-TaON-based samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Figure 2, it can be seen that the single phase of metastable γ-TaON powders with the major peaks at 2θ=23.9°, 27.8°, 31.7°, 37.8° and 46.8°, was successfully obtained after the appropriate nitridation, which is in agreement with the previous report [43]. After the hydrogenation, H-γ-TaON powder with the same major peaks were produced under heat-treatment in hydrogen and argon gases. However, compared to that of γ-TaON, the high intensity of H-γ-TaON powder with high crystallinity was obtained. Especially, there is no obvious change for the crystal phase of CQDs/H-γ-TaON after hybridization with CQDs but the diffraction peak positions of CQDs/H-γ-TaON composites are shifted to slightly lower angles than that of H-γ-TaON, suggesting a strong interaction between H-γ-TaON and CQDs [9,44]. No extra characteristic peaks are detected from the XRD patterns, indicating that there is no impurity in the CQDs/H-γ-TaON products.

The SEM and TEM images of as-synthesized CQDs, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures as well as schematic illustration of the composites are shown in Figure 3. The SEM and TEM images of CQDs/H-γ-TaON sample (Figure 3a and d) indicate that the uniform hollow urchin nanostructures consisting of well-organized nanoneedles with lengths of 100–200nm, are formed. As shown in Figure 3b, the H-γ-TaON hollow urchins with the disordered surface layer are completely crystalline, displaying clearly resolved and well-defined lattice fringes. Compared to that of γ-TaON in the previous work [43], there is no obvious change upon the whole morphology for the CQDs/H-γ-TaON urchins. For the close observation, the high-resolution TEM image in Figure 3e clearly revealed the carbon quantum dots that appear as spherical particles with a diameter of less than 5nm, which is in agreement with the average particle size of the monodispersed CQDs in the range of 2–5nm (Figure 3c) were homogeneously dispersed on the surface of H-γ-TaON hollow urchins, corresponding the relative schematic illustration of CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures (Figure 3f). Moreover, as shown in Figure 3g, the inter-planar spacing of around 0.325nm corresponds to the (002) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon in the hybrid, indicating the formation of CQDs/H-γ-TaON complex structures, benefitting efficient charge separation and electron transfer in the hybrid nanostructures.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to characterize the components and surface properties of Ta, N, O and C in the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures. The full survey spectrum in Figure 4a indicates the presence of carbon (C 1s), tantalum (Ta 4f and 4p), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s) in the γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON composites. In Figure 4b, the two peaks fitted at 26.4 and 28.1eV correspond to the Ta 4f5/2 and Ta 4f7/2 of γ-TaON urchins, respectively. As shown in Figure 4c, the two peaks fitted at 397.1 and 404.9eV are ascribed to the Ta 4p3/2 and N 1s of γ-TaON [15–19,43]. However, the positions of the different peaks of the Ta 4f, Ta 4p and N 1s have shifted to 25.8 and 27.5eV, 396.5 and 404.3eV in the H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON. These shift of binding energies of Ta 4f5/2 and Ta 4f7/2, Ta 4p3/2 and N 1s further confirm the activity of the hydrogen-heat-treatment for the γ-TaON. Especially, the peak at 529.6eV is ascribed to O 1s in the γ-TaON (Figure 4d) while one peak at 529.6eV is deemed as the oxygen bond of Ta–O–Na, while the other located at 531.4eV can be attributed to the O-atoms in the vicinity of O-vacancy in H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON composites [45].

Furthermore, as shown in Figure 5a, the main peak at 284.6eV is attributed to the C–C bond with a sp2 orbital. The peak at 285.9 and 288.4eV are assigned to the C–O and O–C=O bonds, respectively, indicating the existence of carbon quantum dots [36–40]. To further confirm this point, two characteristic peaks located at about 1344.3 and 1599.5cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectra of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON urchin composites (Figure 5b), corresponding to the D-band and the G-band, respectively [12,36–40]. The D-band is associated with vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the plane terminations of disordered graphite, while the G-band is attributed to the ordered graphite structure [46]. Thus, the above-mentioned results have confirmed the formation of CQDs/H-γ-TaON composites.

The optical properties of the hollow urchin materials were measured by obtaining UV–vis and UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 200–700 and 200–1800nm for Ta2O5, γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures, as shown in Figure 6. The absorption band-edge for Ta2O5 and γ-TaON samples are at about 320 and 450nm, corresponding to band gap energies of ca. 3.9 and 2.76eV, respectively (Figure 6a). Although the hydrogenation had little effect on the absorption of γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON exhibited a remarkable absorption in the visible light region, implying suitability for UV–visible photocatalysis due to the formation of surface defects because the oxygen vacancy was introduced in H-γ-TaON by hydrogen treatment, which has been confirmed by XPS spectra in Figure 4d. However, the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures also display obvious visible light absorption ranging from 450 to 700nm. As shown in Figure 6b, in comparison of γ-TaON and H-γ-TaON, the very broad absorption band from visible to near-infrared and large red-shift from the heterostructure arises from the beneficial interaction between CQDs and H-γ-TaON urchins. Together with the intrinsic merits of the hollow urchins, the hydrogenation of γ-TaON and the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructure, this composite system has presented its great potential as a practical photocatalytic material.

To elaborate more details for the promising photocatalyst, the down-converted and up-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs have been conducted by the various excitation wavelengths, as shown in Figure 6c and d. After the excitation range of 350–500nm, the PL peak shifts from 450 (blue) to 560nm (yellow). However, the emissions are located at visible light region in the wavelength range of 400–600nm when the up-converted PL spectra of CQDs with excitations at 750, 850, 950 and 1000nm (NIR light area), demonstrating that CQDs absorb NIR light and then emit visible light due to the apparent up-converted PL behavior, which in turn promotes the transfer of the UV–visible–NIR active charges to the visible light photocatalyst surface [47].

To investigate the recombination of electron–hole pairs in photocatalysts, PL spectra of γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructure were conducted ( Figure 7a). The excitation wavelength is determined as 325nm, and the pristine γ-TaON has a strong emission peak at about 630nm. The lowest PL peak of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructure demonstrates that the recombination of photoexcited carriers is effectively inhibited through the heat-treatment of hydrogen and the modification of CQDs, accelerating the water splitting reaction and improve the charge transferprocesses [9,12]. To gain more insight into the principle via the analysis of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), as shown in Figure 7b, it is obvious that the arch for the γ-TaON electrode was much smaller than that of TaON due to the large impedance of the γ-TaON electrode. The smallest arch of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructure indicates that the hydrogenation and the decoration of the CQDs signficantly reduced the resistance on the movement of charge carriers due to the fast transport of charge carriers.

Currently, Mott–Schottky plots were used to investigate the influence of the hydrogen heat-treatment on the electronic property, in which the flat-band potential and carrier density can be estimated. The tangential line in Mott–Schottky plots is described by:
1 /
C
2
=
(
2 / e
ϵ
r
ϵ
o
N
)
(
E −
E
f
b
– k T / e
)

where C=space charge layers capacitance, e=electron charge, ε r=dielectric constant, ε o=permittivity of vacuum, N=the charge carrier density, and E=applied potential. Using the equation, E fb and N can be calculated from x-intercepts and slope in the plots of C −2 vs. applied external potentials. As shown in Figure 7c and d, the γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON electrodes show a certain flat-band potential value, indicating that there is a negligible effect on the electronic band structure of H-γ-TaON by the hydrogen treatment and CQDs/H-γ-TaON by the decoration of CQDs.

Based on the analysis of Mott–Schottky plots, the space charge layers capacitance has been reduced, indicating that the carrier densities could be increased. Especially, the slope of CQDs/H-γ-TaON electrode is highest, corresponding the order of the slope: CQDs/H-γ-TaON>H-γ-TaON>γ-TaON, indicating that the highest carrier density of CQDs/H-γ-TaON. Since the increased carrier density can increase the degree of band bending by shifting the Fermi level, thus facilitating the charge separation for the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures after the hydrogen heat-treatment and the decoration of the CQDs [29–32].

The photocatalytic performance of the γ-TaON, H-γ-TaON and CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures were evaluated for the degradation of RhB and AO7 solution under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation ( Figure 8). Before the photocatalysis, blank experiments were performed in the absence of catalysts, indicating that there is no obvious change for the photocatalysis. As shown in Figure 8, the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies (PDE) of RhB and AO7 over CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures is more than 98% (140min) and 99% (100min), respectively, under UV light irradiation. Especially, the UV-light-driven photocatalytic degradation activity of CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures is superior to that of H-γ-TaON (80% for RhB and 90% for AO7) and γ-TaON (64% for RhB and 80% for AO7). Under visible light irradiation, the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures exhibited the highest photocatalytic property for RhB (PDE=88% within 140min) and AO7 (PDE=90% within 100min) degradation. Moreover, the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures presented the highest photocatalytic property for RhB (PDE=61% within 140min) and AO7 (PDE=66% within 100min) degradation under NIR light irradiation, corresponding higher photocatalytic activities than that of H-γ-TaON (30% for RhB and 50% for AO7) and γ-TaON (22% for RhB and 34% for AO7). In comparison, γ-TaON presented the higher photocatalytic activities than β-TaON (Figure S1). In the dark, the concentration of pollutants by use of the pure γ-TaON hollow urchins as photocatalyst without any irradiation has been decreased due to the absorption rather than photocatalytic degradation because the hollow architectures with high surface area could absorb the pollutants molecular [43]. However, there is no obvious change for the photocatalytic performance of the γ-TaON sample under NIR light irradiation because the pure TaON samples can not absorb NIR light. Currently, the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures exhibit the stable photocatalytic performance under UV, visible, and near-infrared light irradiation (Figures S2 and S3). Based on above-mentioned results, it can be seen that the photocatalytic degradation performance of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures is higher than that of γ-TaON and H-γ-TaON. Thus, the improved UV and visible photocatalytic property of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures can be attributed to due to the high surface area from hollow nanostructures, the strong light scattering, the anisotropic charge transport and reduced charge transfer resistance based UV–visible–NIR, photoluminescence, electrochemical impedance and Mott–Schottky analysis (Figures 6 and 7). Especially, the NIR photocatalytic activity is from the efficient photo-induced electron transfer, electron reservoir, and photoluminescence properties of CQDs, which can absorb NIR light and convert into visible light and transfer to visible light photocatalytic H-γ-TaON [47]. The cooperating amplification effects of morphology innovation, hydrogenation and broad spectrum heterojunctions, provide more valuable insights for creating more purpose-designed (oxy)nitride photocatalysts.

To further demonstrate the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures, the hydrogen evolution was conducted for the photocatalytic water splitting, as shown in Figure 9. The CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures exhibited the highest hydrogen production rate of about496.5 µmolh−1, which is higher than that of γ-TaON (395µmolh−1) and H-γ-TaON (415µmolh−1) urchins due to the synergistic effect from the hydrogen treatment and the decoration of the CQDs. Especially, an apparent quantum efficiency of 12.2% of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow heterostructures was achieved under 420nm light, which was about 61 times higher than that of the conventional TaON [48]. Therefore, with regard to the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures, the H-γ-TaON urchin is expected to act as an efficient light harvester and the heterostructure provides the efficient charge separation to suppress the electron–hole recombination [49], which greatly enhances the photocatalytic activity in the hydrogen production.

To investigate the stability of the photocatalysts, cyclic performance toward the photocatalytic hydrogen generation through consecutive five test cycles was carried out over the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructure as a representative nanocomposite. As depicted in Figure 9b, the CQDs/H-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterostructures maintained its high photoactivity for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution after five cycles under identical experimental conditions. Evidently, there was no significant change in the photocatalytic hydrogen production after several cycles under visible light illumination, demonstrating that the developed photocatalyst was stable with the prolonged reaction duration. Based on the XRD analyses, no substantial changes in the phase and chemical structure of the CQDs/H-γ-TaON sample were observed after five successive cycles (Figure S4), confirming high durability of the photocatalyst.

This photocatalytic mechanism, as shown in Figure 10, is proposed for the roles of CQDs in the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterostructures during the photocatalytic process under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation. Firstly, CQDs can function as electron collectors and transporters to trap electrons and transfer electrons generated from H-γ-TaON hollow urchins due to UV–visible-light irradiation, hence preventing the recombination of electron–hole pairs, and consequently enhancing the charge separation and photocatalytic performance in the CQDs/H-γ-TaON composites [36–40]. Secondly, the CQDs can absorb longer wavelength light and then emit shorter wavelength light due to up-converted behavior, which can in turn excite the H-γ-TaON to form electron–hole pairs, effectively harnessing the broad spectrum of sunshine to improve the NIR photocatalytic properties [26]. Currently, the oxidation reaction happens on the surface of H-γ-TaON for the efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Therefore, the synergetic catalytic effect through the interfacial modulation and charge-transfer channels designing by use of the hydrogen treatment and the decoration of the CQDs upon the enhancement of practically photocatalytic applications.

In summary, we develop a simple strategy to fabricate the dispersed carbon quantum dots deposited on the hydrogenated-γ-TaON hollow urchin heterojunctions with excellent broad spectrum photocatalytic property. The grazing incidence XRD, SEM, TEM, XPS and Raman techniques indicate the formation of CQDs on the H-γ-TaON hollow urchins. Novel CQDs/H-γ-TaON photocatalysts with broad spectrum have harnessed UV, visible, and near-infrared light to decompose organic contaminants in aqueous solution. Moreover, efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production as high as 496.5µmolh−1 with an apparent quantum efficiency of 12.2% under 420nm irradiation (about 61 times higher than that of conventional TaON) was achieved over CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunction. Combining ultraviolet–visible absorption, photoluminescence, electrochemical impedance and Mott–Schottky analysis, the efficient UV–visible–NIR photocatalytic property can attribute to the efficient charge transfer. Moreover, the hollow urchin heterojunctions with high surface area and superior transport property offer a well-behaving architectures, which in turn promotes the transfer of the UV–visible–NIR active charges to the photocatalyst surface to participate in photocatalytic reactions. By extending the optical absorption towards near-infrared region and building the unique heterojunction, the photocatalytic efficiencies of pollutants degradation and hydrogen evolution for the CQDs/H-γ-TaON heterojunctions have been realized. The concept of broad-spectrum active photocatalysts should be useful for the future design of robust semiconductors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51472027), Beijing High School Youth Talent Plan (YETP0351) and National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, No. 2013CB632404). J.G. Hou thanks the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) for a support in the form of a fellowship tenable at Tohoku University, Sendai, Japan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.nanoen.2015.09.005.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jungang Hou received his PhD degree (2010) in materials science from Tianjin University, China. He joined the faculty of University of Science and Technology Beijing and was promoted to associate professor in 2013. He worked in Tohoku University as a fellow of Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) from 2014 to 2015. Since 2015, he has joined the faculty of Dalian University of Technology as professor. His current research interests include semiconductor photocatalysis, photocatalytic hydrogen production, CO2 reduction to hydrocarbon fuels and synthesis and applications of nanostructured materials.

Huijie Cheng received her PhD degree in materials science from Tianjin University, China. She is currently working as a postdoctoral researcher in University of Science and Technology Beijing. Her current research interests include semiconductor photocatalysis and photocatalytic hydrogen production, synthesis and applications of nanostructured materials.

Chao Yang received his B.S. degree (2013) in University of Science and Technology Beijing, China. Since 2014 he joined in Erasmus Mundus Joint Master Program-EuroPhotonics as a master student. From 2014 to 2015, he studied in Institute Fresnel, Aix-Marseille University, France. Since 2015, he is currently a M.S. degree in Karlsruhe School of Optics and Photonics, Karlsruhe Institute of Technology, Germany. His current research interests include solar energy conversion of semiconductor photocatalysts.

Osamu Takeda received his PhD degree (2006) in Graduate School of Engineering from the University of Tokyo, Japan. He joined the faculty of Department of Metallurgy, Graduate School of Engineering, Tohoku University in Japan as an Assistant Professor in 2006. His current research interests include high temperature materials processing, measurements on thermophysical properties of high temperature melt, and electrochemistry on semiconductor photocatalyst.

Prof. Hongmin Zhu received his PhD degree (1989) in Tohoku University, Japan. He joined the faculty of Tohoku University as associate professor in Tohoku University in 1989, and worked as a visiting scientist at Massachusetts Institute of Technology from 1997 to 2000, and in Norwegian University of Science and Technology from 2000 to 2001. Since 2001, he joined the faculty of University of Science and Technology Beijing as professor. Since 2013, he joined the faculty of Tohoku University as professor. His current research interests include materials physical chemistry, metallurgical processing, and synthesis of functional materials including semiconductor, battery materials, photocatalyst and thermoelectric materials.
